text: The surgeon inserts a reusable D-port into the anus and utilizes curved reusable instruments to perform a transanal laparoscopic repair of an early rectovaginal fistula.
The surgeon performs lavage of the cavity and explores the lower rectum, revealing a large defect between twelve and three o'clock that connects the lower rectum to the vagina.

The surgeon inserts a Vicryl 2-0 suture through the central port orifice. A monocurve reusable needle holder is introduced at the three o'clock position, while monocurve grasping forceps are positioned at the nine o'clock position. Figure-of-eight sutures are applied to close the defect, starting from the inferior edge and then moving to the superior edge.
The surgeon estimates the defect to be two centimeters in size and located twelve centimeters from the anal margin. An intraluminal knotting technique is employed to secure the suture.

The surgeon operates using curved instruments to avoid clashing, cutting the suture with monocurved scissors and removing it through the port orifice at the nine o'clock position. Additional sutures are placed using the same technique.

The surgeon inspects the closure defects and identifies an improperly closed superior edge, placing an additional suture to secure it. The procedure continues with ergonomic intraluminal knotting using curved instruments.
The surgeon performs a final injection of methylene blue through the vagina, confirming no passage of the dye into the lower rectum, indicating successful closure of the defect.
